mnemonics.title: SUPER for Supervised Learning
mnemonics.content:
S - Supervised uses
U - Labeled data (gUided)
P - Predicts outputs
E - Examples: Classification, Regression
R - Requires teacher (labels)

mnemonics.title: UN-UN for Unsupervised
mnemonics.content:
U - Unlabeled data
N - No teacher/guides
- - Finds hidden patterns
U - Unsupervised
N - Needs no labels

mnemonics.title: Classification vs Regression
mnemonics.content:
C - Categorical (discrete classes)
R - Real-valued (continuous numbers)
C comes before R alphabetically as classification comes before regression in learning complexity

acronyms.usage: The broader field of enabling computers to learn from data
Enables systems to improve performance on tasks without explicit programming
Dimensionality reduction technique that transforms features into principal components
Algorithm that classifies based on K closest training examples
Common regression evaluation metric measuring average squared prediction errors

visualTips.visualization: Draw two parallel flows: Left flow (Supervised) shows Data → Labels → Model → Predictions. Right flow (Unsupervised) shows Data → No Labels → Model → Patterns/Groups.
Imagine scatter plot with two colored regions (red/blue). Classification finds the line/curve that best separates the two classes.
Think of K-Means as placing K flags on a field; each data point runs to the nearest flag, flags then reposition to average location of their runners, repeat until flags stop moving.
Draw three curves: (1) Straight line missing data pattern (underfit), (2) Wavy line touching all points (overfit), (3) Smooth curve capturing trend (optimal fit).

analogies.analogy: Like a student learning with a textbook that has answers in the back. The model learns from labeled examples (questions + correct answers).
Like a child learning to group toys by color/shape without being told categories. The model discovers natural groupings itself.
Like sorting mail into different bins (spam, inbox, promotions) based on features of each piece of mail.
Like estimating the price of a used car based on its mileage, age, and condition - predicting a continuous value.
Like a party host instinctively grouping guests by interests even without being told - discovering natural groupings.
Like summarizing a long article into key bullet points - capturing essential information in fewer dimensions.
